title: Exodus 40
subtitle: God Commands Erection; Moses Obeys; Glory Fills the Tabernacle

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — God Commands the Erection; Moses Obeys

notes.note: MacArthur: Set up the tabernacle, the tent of meeting, on the first day of the first month. The tabernacle's erection is dated exactly one year after the Exodus began. The covenant community's first anniversary is marked by the installation of God's dwelling among them.

MacArthur: Moses did everything just as the LORD commanded him. The single sentence summarising the entire tabernacle construction and erection. Faithfulness to divine instruction is the standard; Moses achieved it.

heb.paragraph: V.17-19: "On the first day of the first month of the second year, the tabernacle was set up (hûqam)." The verb qûm (to rise, to set up, to establish) marks the moment of Tabernacle inauguration. The same verb describes the establishment of covenants (Gen 17:7) and the resurrection of the dead. The Tabernacle being "raised up" on the first day of the first month (New Year's Day) was a new year, a new covenant, a new beginning — ordered life recommencing around the divine Presence.

V.34-38: "Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. Moses could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle." The cloud (ʿānān) that had guided Israel from Egypt (13:21-22) now settled as permanent occupant of the Tabernacle. The divine Presence that had been mobile and external became resident and interior — a movement Jesus fulfills when the Spirit comes to dwell within believers (John 14:17).

hist.historical: The erection of the tabernacle on the first day of the first month of the second year (v. 17) — exactly one year after the Exodus — creates a liturgical calendar marker. New Year temple dedications were standard in Mesopotamian religion; the Akitu festival involved the ritual re-establishment of divine presence in the temple. Moses personally performs every act of assembly, anointing, and consecration, functioning as the mediator between God's instructions and their earthly realisation.
hist.context: The command to erect the Tabernacle on the first of the first month creates a precise one-year symmetry: Israel left Egypt on the fifteenth of Nisan; the Tabernacle stands on the first of Nisan, twelve months later. The year of wilderness formation — plagues, Passover, Red Sea, Sinai, the calf, the covenant renewal — culminates in God moving in. The entire year's journey was preparation for this moment.

refs.note: "The Word became flesh and dwelt (tabernacled) among us, and we have seen his glory." The Tabernacle's completion is the OT foreshadowing of the Incarnation.
"God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them." The ultimate fulfilment of Exod 40.
Christ entered the greater and more perfect tabernacle — the earthly is surpassed by the heavenly.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes the glory filling the tabernacle (40:34–35) is the book's climactic act — the goal announced in 25:8 ('that I may dwell among them') is achieved. The cloud and glory that descended on Sinai (24:15–18) now descend on the covenant community's portable sanctuary.

Alter notes the book began with Israel unable to cry out, groaning under oppression, with no visible divine presence. It ends with the glory so intense that even Moses cannot enter the tent — the divine presence overwhelming the space prepared for it. The journey from slavery to divine dwelling is complete.

Calvin: 'By this visible symbol God declared that he took up his dwelling in the midst of the people. This was the fulfilment of the promise, I will dwell among you. The glory was an accommodation to human capacity — a visible pledge of the invisible presence.'

The NET note connects this to 1 Kgs 8:10–11 (Solomon's temple dedication) and Ezek 43:4–5 (glory returning to the temple in Ezekiel's vision). The cloud-and-glory pattern — divine arrival at the legitimate sanctuary — is the recurring signature of covenant fulfilment in the OT.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter notes the book began with Israel unable to cry out, groaning under oppression, with no visible divine presence. It ends with the glory so intense that even Moses cannot enter the tent — the divine presence overwhelming the space prepared for it. The journey from slavery to divine dwelling is complete.

Calvin: 'By this visible symbol God declared that he took up his dwelling in the midst of the people. This was the fulfilment of the promise, I will dwell among you. The glory was an accommodation to human capacity — a visible pledge of the invisible presence.'

The NET note connects this to 1 Kgs 8:10–11 (Solomon's temple dedication) and Ezek 43:4–5 (glory returning to the temple in Ezekiel's vision). The cloud-and-glory pattern — divine arrival at the legitimate sanctuary — is the recurring signature of covenant fulfilment in the OT.

Calvin: 'By this visible symbol God declared that he took up his dwelling in the midst of the people. This was the fulfilment of the promise, I will dwell among you. The glory was an accommodation to human capacity — a visible pledge of the invisible presence.'

The NET note connects this to 1 Kgs 8:10–11 (Solomon's temple dedication) and Ezek 43:4–5 (glory returning to the temple in Ezekiel's vision). The cloud-and-glory pattern — divine arrival at the legitimate sanctuary — is the recurring signature of covenant fulfilment in the OT.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note connects this to 1 Kgs 8:10–11 (Solomon's temple dedication) and Ezek 43:4–5 (glory returning to the temple in Ezekiel's vision). The cloud-and-glory pattern — divine arrival at the legitimate sanctuary — is the recurring signature of covenant fulfilment in the OT.

The NET note on the glory filling the tabernacle so that Moses could not enter discusses the irony: the covenant mediator who received the tabernacle's instructions on the mountain cannot enter the completed structure. The glory exceeds even Moses. The note traces the parallel to 1 Kgs 8:10-11 and Ezek 43:4-5 — the glory that fills sanctuaries and the eschatological temple.

sections.header: Verses 17–33 — The Tabernacle Erected; Moses Finishes the Work

poi.name: The Tabernacle — central position in camp
poi.text: The Tabernacle is erected at the centre of the Israelite camp (Numbers 2 will describe the camp arrangement). The covenant dwelling is at the geographic centre of the community — all tribal life oriented around it. The wilderness camp is the Promised Land in portable, provisional form.

notes.note: MacArthur: The glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. The glory cloud that filled the tabernacle (1 Kgs 8:11 at the Temple's dedication; John 1:14 at the Incarnation) is the covenant goal achieved.

MacArthur: Whenever the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle, the Israelites would set out; but if the cloud did not lift, they did not set out. The ending of Exodus is not arrival but departure -- the portable God leads the travelling people.

heb.paragraph: V.34-38: The kābôd (glory) of the LORD that filled the completed Tabernacle is the climax of the entire book of Exodus. The same glory had appeared at the Red Sea (14:4), on Sinai (24:16), and now settled permanently in the midst of the people. This inaugurating glory-filling will be repeated at Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11) and its absence will mark the Exile (Ezek 10-11). Its return in fullness is the eschatological promise: "the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea" (Hab 2:14).

V.36-38: "In all the travels of the Israelites, whenever the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle, they would set out; but if the cloud did not lift, they did not set out — until the day it lifted." The entire movement of the wilderness community was governed by the cloud. ʿal-pî YHWH ("at the mouth/command of the LORD") was the governing principle of Israel's travel: not military calculation, seasonal timing, or human decision, but simple responsiveness to divine direction. This is the model of the Spirit-led life.

hist.historical: The systematic order of erection — ark first, then table, lampstand, incense altar, outer furnishings — moves from the most sacred outward, the reverse of the approach pattern for worshippers. Each item is placed 'as the LORD commanded Moses' — the phrase occurs eight times in this chapter alone. The anointing of all furnishings with sacred oil consecrates them from common to holy use, a transition fundamental to ancient Near Eastern temple theology.
hist.context: The erection narrative (vv.17–33) repeats "as the Lord commanded him" with each element — six more times in addition to v.16. The total across Exod 39–40 reaches the theological number. Moses is meticulous: every furnishing placed, every lamp lit, every piece of bread set out, every offering made. The book's final verse of human action is "so Moses finished the work" — the same phrase used of God at creation (Gen 2:2).

refs.note: When Solomon's temple is completed, the cloud fills it so that the priests cannot perform their service — the same glory phenomenon. The tabernacle's glory transfers to the temple.
John writes that the Word 'became flesh and made his dwelling (eskēnōsen, lit. tabernacled) among us, and we have seen his glory.' The glory-cloud in Exodus 40 becomes incarnation in John 1.

Revelation's climax — 'God's dwelling place is now among the people' — completes the arc begun here. Tabernacle → temple → incarnation → new creation: the glory that filled the tent at Sinai fills all reality.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The erection account's repetition of 'just as the LORD commanded Moses' (seven times) creates the creation-week framework. Sarna notes each element placed in the tabernacle mirrors each day of creation — the tabernacle's completion is a new creation's consummation.

The glory filling the tabernacle is the book's climax because it fulfils the purpose stated in 29:45: 'I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God.' Sarna notes the Exodus narrative is not ultimately about liberation from Egypt but about this moment — the God of all creation taking up residence among his covenant people.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The meticulous placement sequence — altar, laver, tent, Ark, veil, table, lampstand, incense altar — reverses the construction order (inside-out vs. outside-in). Alter notes the placement order is the worshipper's approach order: altar first, presence last.

The book's final image — the cloud of glory by day and fire by night, guiding the Israelites through all their journeys — returns to the cloud and fire first mentioned in 13:21-22. Alter notes the structural closure: the guidance that began at the Exodus continues from the tabernacle. The portable divine dwelling is the Exodus compressed into an ongoing covenant presence.

Calvin: 'Moses, as a faithful and diligent servant, performed all that was commanded him. There is no part of the service of God more acceptable than this — the precise observance of what he has prescribed, without adding anything of our own.'

Calvin: The filling of the tabernacle with divine glory is the ultimate answer to the golden calf's question: where is our God? God is here — not in a golden idol made by human hands but in the covenant dwelling constructed by Spirit-empowered obedience to divine specification. The glory that filled the tabernacle vindicates the entire construction enterprise.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'first month, first day' identifies this as the new year of the second year since the Exodus. The tabernacle's erection on the first day of the new year creates a calendar reset — the new year of Israel's covenant life begins with the establishment of God's dwelling.

The NET note on the cloud and fire guiding the Israelites' journeys discusses the structural function of the closing verses: they are both a narrative conclusion and a transitional bridge to the wilderness journey that Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy will narrate. The book ends in motion — the tabernacle is not a permanent building but a portable guidance system.

sections.header: Verses 34–38 — The Glory of the Lord Fills the Tabernacle

tl.name: Tabernacle erected — 1st day of 1st month, 2nd year — this passage
tl.text: Exactly 9 months after arriving at Sinai, the Tabernacle is completed and erected. God fills it with his glory. The book of Exodus ends with the covenant dwelling operational — the goal announced in Exodus 25:8 achieved.

tl.name: Conquest of Canaan begins; 40-year wilderness ends

notes.note: MacArthur: Moses could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it. Even Moses cannot enter when the glory fills it. This anticipates the need for a greater Mediator -- one who is himself the embodiment of the divine glory (Heb 1:3).

MacArthur: The glory filling the tabernacle is the covenant's fulfilment: God dwells with his people in the midst of their camp. Moses cannot enter — not as rejection but as the sign that the glory has fully occupied the space. The cloud and fire that will guide Israel through all subsequent journeys is the mobile divine presence that the entire construction project was designed to house.

heb.paragraph: V.34-35: The tabernacle was "filled" (māleʾ) with the glory of the LORD — so completely that Moses could not enter. The same verb describes: the Spirit filling Bezalel (31:3); the earth filled with God's glory (Num 14:21); the Temple filled at dedication (1 Kgs 8:10). The eschatological vision is the whole earth filled with divine glory (Isa 6:3; Hab 2:14). The Tabernacle is the first down-payment on that promise: God fills what is consecrated and offered.

V.1: "The LORD said to Moses..."  The book of Exodus opens with God's people in silence under oppression and closes with God speaking continuously, directing every movement of the liberated community. The lēʾmōr ("saying") that drives 40+ divine speeches throughout Exodus charts the transformation: from a people who cried out and were heard, to a people who move in response to God's own continuous word.

hist.historical: The cloud covering the tent and the glory (kābôd) of the LORD filling the tabernacle is the climactic moment of the entire book. The cloud-and-fire theophany that guided Israel from Egypt now takes up permanent residence. This 'dwelling' (šākan, the root of šĕkînâ, later 'Shekinah') fulfils the purpose stated in 25:8: 'Let them make a sanctuary for me, and I will dwell among them.' The guiding cloud — present by day, fire by night — transforms the tabernacle from a static building into a mobile divine throne, moving with Israel through the wilderness. The book of Exodus ends not with arrival but with readiness for the journey ahead.
hist.context: The glory filling the Tabernacle (vv.34–35) is the climax of the entire book of Exodus — indeed the climax of the entire Pentateuch's narrative of covenant relationship. God descends into the dwelling his people built for him. The cloud that led them out of Egypt now leads from within. The fire that burned at Sinai now burns at the centre of the camp. The covenant promise — "I will dwell among them" (29:45) — is fulfilled.

refs.note: Solomon's Temple: when it is completed, the same cloud fills the temple and the priests cannot stand to minister. The same glory that filled the Tabernacle fills the Temple.
"The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we have seen his glory." The Incarnation is the personal fulfilment of Exod 40:34.
The New Jerusalem has no temple "because the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple." The Tabernacle's glory is finally universalised — God's presence fills everything, not just one tent.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes the glory filling the tabernacle is the book's climactic act — the goal announced in 25:8 achieved. The cloud and glory that descended on Sinai (24:15–18) now descend on the portable sanctuary. The Exodus's purpose is fulfilled.

Sarna notes the glory filling the tabernacle is the exact reversal of the golden calf episode. At Horeb, Israel tried to make a god that would go before them (32:1); now the true God dwells among them and leads them. The tabernacle does not represent God — it houses his presence. The covenant community has what they sought by the wrong means.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter notes the book began with Israel unable to cry out and ends with God so fully present that Moses cannot enter the tent. The reversal is complete. The journey from slavery to divine dwelling is the Exodus's arc — and it ends here, with the divine arrival.

The book's very last words — 'throughout all their journeys' — are Exodus's own self-summary. Alter notes that Exodus is a book about a journey: from slavery to Sinai, from Sinai to the wilderness, from the wilderness to Canaan. The guidance that fills the tabernacle will attend every stage of that unfinished journey.

Calvin: 'The cloud covered the tabernacle and the glory of the LORD filled it — not as if God were circumscribed within any space, but he thus showed by outward sign that he chose that place for the dwelling of his grace.'

Calvin: The God who filled the temple of Solomon with his glory (1 Kgs 8:10) and the eschatological temple with his glory (Ezek 43:4-5) is the same God who here fills the wilderness tabernacle. The tabernacle-filling is the paradigmatic event; all subsequent sanctuary-fillings derive their meaning from this first dwelling.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note connects the glory-filling to 1 Kgs 8:10–11 (Solomon's temple) and Ezek 43:4–5 (the eschatological temple). The cloud-and-glory pattern is the recurring signature of covenant fulfilment in the OT. Exodus 40 is the paradigmatic instance from which all later instances draw their meaning.

The NET note connects the three canonical glory-fillings: the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35), Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11), and the eschatological temple (Ezek 43:4-5). The note identifies the cloud-and-glory pattern as the canonical signature of covenant fulfilment: when God's dwelling is complete and acceptable, his glory fills it.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: LORD is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: Context  The command to erect the Tabernacle on the first of the first month creates a precise one-year symmetry: Israel left Egypt on the fifteenth of Nisan…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle. And Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it, and the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle."NIV"Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle. Moses could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle."

src.title: Temple Dedication Parallels
src.quote: Ancient Near Eastern temple dedication texts describe the deity's arrival into the completed temple — the god "entering" the new house. The Tabernacle's glory-filling fits this pattern but with a radical difference: the God of Israel arrives uninvited, spontaneously, in overwhelming glory that even Moses cannot withstand.
src.note: God's arrival in the Tabernacle is sovereign and unmanageable — not the controlled "entrance" of a deity into a managed ritual.

src.title: Cloud and Fire
src.quote: The pillar of cloud and fire appeared at the Exodus (13:21) and now closes the book at the Tabernacle (40:38). The literary inclusio frames the entire Exodus narrative: cloud and fire from departure to dwelling.
src.note: The cloud and fire form a literary bracket around the entire book.

rec.text: Solomon's Temple dedication: the same cloud fills the temple; the priests cannot minister. The Tabernacle's glory-filling is the template for all subsequent divine indwelling in the OT.

The supreme NT interpretation: "The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we have seen his glory." Jesus is the ultimate Tabernacle — God dwelling with humanity in a way that exceeds even Moses's face-to-face relationship. In Christ, the glory can be seen without consuming.

rows.label: The Glory Fills the Tabernacle
rows.text: Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. Moses could not enter because the cloud had settled on it. The divine glory that fills the tabernacle is the book's climax: the purpose stated in 29:45 is fulfilled.

rows.label: The Journey Continues: Cloud and Fire
rows.text: Throughout all their journeys, whenever the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle, the Israelites set out. The book ends in motion -- the tabernacle is not a permanent building but the portable guarantee of divine presence on the continuing journey.

hebtext.note: V.17-19: "On the first day of the first month of the second year, the tabernacle was set up (hûqam)." The verb qûm (to rise, to set up, to establish) marks the moment of Tabernacle inauguration. The same verb describes the establishment of covenants (Gen 17:7) and the resurrection of the dead. The Tabernacle being "raised up" on the first day of the first month (New Year's Day) was a new year, a new covenant, a new beginning — ordered life recommencing around the divine Presence.

V.34-38: "Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. Moses could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle." The cloud (ʿānān) that had guided Israel from Egypt (13:21-22) now settled as permanent occupant of the Tabernacle. The divine Presence that had been mobile and external became resident and interior — a movement Jesus fulfills when the Spirit comes to dwell within believers (John 14:17).

V.34-38: The kābôd (glory) of the LORD that filled the completed Tabernacle is the climax of the entire book of Exodus. The same glory had appeared at the Red Sea (14:4), on Sinai (24:16), and now settled permanently in the midst of the people. This inaugurating glory-filling will be repeated at Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11) and its absence will mark the Exile (Ezek 10-11). Its return in fullness is the eschatological promise: "the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea" (Hab 2:14).

V.36-38: "In all the travels of the Israelites, whenever the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle, they would set out; but if the cloud did not lift, they did not set out — until the day it lifted." The entire movement of the wilderness community was governed by the cloud. ʿal-pî YHWH ("at the mouth/command of the LORD") was the governing principle of Israel's travel: not military calculation, seasonal timing, or human decision, but simple responsiveness to divine direction. This is the model of the Spirit-led life.

V.34-35: The tabernacle was "filled" (māleʾ) with the glory of the LORD — so completely that Moses could not enter. The same verb describes: the Spirit filling Bezalel (31:3); the earth filled with God's glory (Num 14:21); the Temple filled at dedication (1 Kgs 8:10). The eschatological vision is the whole earth filled with divine glory (Isa 6:3; Hab 2:14). The Tabernacle is the first down-payment on that promise: God fills what is consecrated and offered.

The book of Exodus opens with God's people in silence under oppression and closes with God speaking continuously, directing every movement of the liberated community. The lēʾmōr ("saying") that drives 40+ divine speeches throughout Exodus charts the transformation: from a people who cried out and were heard, to a people who move in response to God's own continuous word.

"God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them." The ultimate fulfilment of Exod 40.
Christ entered the greater and more perfect tabernacle — the earthly is surpassed by the heavenly.
Solomon's Temple: when it is completed, the same cloud fills the temple and the priests cannot stand to minister. The same glory that filled the Tabernacle fills the Temple.

tx.title: Erection date — 'first month of the second year'
tx.content: MT dates the tabernacle erection to 'the first day of the first month of the second year.' The LXX agrees precisely. The SP agrees. This chronological notice anchors the entire priestly chronological framework and is textually secure across all witnesses.
tx.note: The date's stability is crucial because the priestly chronology of the wilderness period depends on it. Any alteration would cascade through Numbers and beyond.

tx.title: Glory filling the tabernacle — narrative conclusion
tx.content: MT's conclusion — the glory cloud filling the tabernacle — is preserved identically across MT, SP, and LXX. The parallel in 1 Kings 8:10–11 (Solomon's temple) uses similar language. No textual witness alters the climactic moment. Targum Onqelos adds 'the Glory of the LORD's Shekinah.'
tx.note: The narrative arc from Exodus 25:8 ('let them make me a sanctuary that I may dwell among them') to 40:34 ('the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle') is the theological spine of the book, preserved without variant.

debate.title: Why Does Exodus End with the Glory and Not with Entry into Canaan?

positions.name: The Exodus's goal is divine dwelling, not territorial possession
positions.argument: The book ends where its purpose statement said it would (29:45: 'I will dwell among the Israelites'). The land is the next chapter; the covenant goal — divine presence among the covenant people — is achieved here.

positions.name: Editorial decision to separate Exodus from Numbers
positions.argument: The Pentateuch's division into five books is a later editorial decision. The natural narrative continuation is the journey to Canaan. Exodus 40 is an artificial stopping point.

coaching.tip: Moses erects the tabernacle on the first day of the first month (v. 17)—New Year, new worship. Exodus began with calendar reset (12:2); it ends with worship established. Time and liturgy interlock.
'The glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle' (v. 34). Moses cannot enter—the glory is too intense. What began at a burning bush ends in blazing presence. God has moved in. Mission accomplished.
